column1:
Which of the following veins has a direct connection to both superficial and deep veins?

A: popliteal vein and inferior vena cava
B: brachial vein and small saphenous vein
C: femoral vein and axillary vein
D: subclavian and popliteal vein
column2:
Answer: D. subclavian and popliteal vein

Feedback:
The subclavian vein receives blood from the cephalic vein and the axillary vein. The popliteal vein receives blood from the small
saphenous and deep calf veins.

column1:
The weight of the column of blood within the veins when standing is called?

A: Systemic Pressure
B: Hydrostatic Pressure
C: Pulmonary Pressure
D: Transmural Pressure
column2:
Answer: B. Hydrostatic Pressure

Feedback:
The systemic blood pressure is related to the arterial pressures within the body. The pulmonary pressure is related to the
pressure within the pulmonary arteries.

column1:
An effective calf pump causes ________________________ in the pressure in the deep veins of the calf.

A: little change
B: an increase
C: a decrease
D: an increase in cardiac output and an increase
column2:
Answer: C. a decrease

Feedback:
Effective calf pump reduces blood stasis/pooling, decreases venous volume and pressure in the calf veins. Ineffective calf pump
leads to increased stasis, venous volume and pressure in the calf veins; reflux is a contributing factor

column1:
If the image displayed is a transverse view of the posterior right lower leg, Letter B correspond to:

A: lateral gastrocnemius veins
B: peroneal veins
C: medial gastrocnemius veins
D: posterior tibial veins
column2:
Answer: A. lateral gastrocnemius veins

Feedback:
When imaging the posterior leg, the notch on the transducer should be placed toward the right side of the patient, just like
imaging the anterior leg. The right side of the image is lateral and the left side of the image is medial in both imaging views.
A. small saphenous vein
B. lateral gastrocnemius veins
C. medial gastrocnemius veins
D. popliteal vein

column1:
Normal perforator valves allow blood flow in the perforator to move:

A: from the femoral vein to the GSV
B: toward the heart
C: from the GSV to femoral vein
D: toward the ankle
column2:
Answer: C. from the GSV to femoral vein

Feedback:
Normal perforator valves allow blood flow in the perforator to move from the superficial system to the deep system. Dilated
perforators will allow bidirectional blood blow.

column1:
The innominate veins course ____________ to the innominate artery.

A: anterior and lateral
B: posterior and lateral
C: posterior and medial
D: inferior and posterior
column2:
Answer: A. anterior and lateral

Feedback:
The innominate veins course anterior and lateral to the innominate artery. There is a right and a left innominate vein, but only 1
innominate artery (aortic arch origin). The innominate vessels are also referred to as the brachiocephalic vessels.

column1:
The left and right innominate veins merge to form the ____________________.

A: inferior vena cava (IVC)
B: Right atrium
C: superior vena cava (SVC)
D: Brachiocephalic vein
column2:
Answer: C. superior vena cava (SVC)

Feedback:
The right and left innominate veins merge to form the SVC.

column1:
Which calf vessel's anatomic course has been referred to as a "stocking seam"?

A: posterior tibial vein (PTV)
B: small saphenous vein (SSV)
C: great saphenous vein (GSV)
D: superficial femoral vein (SFV)
column2:
Answer: B. small saphenous vein (SSV)

Feedback:
The small saphenous vein originates very superficially in the distal posterior calf. It courses midline up the calf to reach the
popliteal vein at or near the popliteal fossa. Its course has been said to resemble the seam of a type of pantyhose or stockings.

column1:
How can you differentiate the anterior accessory saphenous vein (AASV) from the great saphenous vein (GSV)?

A: the AASV courses through the lateral thigh and the GSV courses through the medial thigh
B: the AASV travels in the saphenous compartment but the GSV does not
C: the GSV travels in the saphenous compartment but the AASV does not
D: look for the alignment sign that is only associated with the AASV
column2:
Answer: D. look for the alignment sign that is only associated with the AASV

Feedback:
Alignment Sign - the AASV is aligned with the femoral artery and it courses anterior to the femoral artery within the saphenous
compartment. This characteristic differentiates it from the GSV because the GSV is medial to the femoral artery/vein in the
saphenous compartment.

column1:
During inspiration the flow in the veins of the upper extremities _____________.

A: increases
B: decreases
C: remains unchanged
D: reverses direction
column2:
Answer: A. increases

Feedback:
During inspiration the pressure within the chest cavity decreases allowing venous flow from the upper extremities to increase.
During expiration, the pressure within the chest cavity increases causing the venous flow from the upper extremities to
decrease. THE OPPOSITE IS TRUE WITH THE FLOW FROM THE LOWER EXTREMITIES.
During inspiration the pressure within the abdominal cavity increases causing venous flow from the lower extremities to
decrease. During expiration, the pressure within the abdominal cavity decreases allowing the venous flow from the lower
extremities to increase.

column1:
Which of the following describes a normal lower extremity response to the Valsalva maneuver?

A: flow in the subclavian vein will be augmented toward the heart with slight reversal upon release
B: the femoral vein will demonstrate cardiac pulsatility
C: flow in the iliac vein will reverse for at least 1 second before normalizing
D: flow in the femoral vein will stop at onset of the maneuver
column2:
Answer: D. flow in the femoral vein will stop at onset of the maneuver

Feedback:
The Valsalva maneuver will significantly decrease or stop flow toward the heart in the deep veins of the legs. Upon release of
the maneuver, flow will be briefly augmented toward the heart and then normalize.

column1:
When transmural pressure in a vein is high:

A: the associated artery will flatten and flow velocity increases
B: the vein becomes flattened and dumbbell shaped
C: the vein becomes enlarged and rounded
D: the associated artery will distend and flow velocity decreases
column2:
Answer: C. the vein becomes enlarged and rounded

Feedback:
Transmural pressure is the pressure within the veins pushing outward and is normally low. It increases with increasing venous
volume. When the pressure inside the vein is high compared to the surrounding pressure outside the vein, the vessel distends.
When the pressure inside the vein is low compared to the surrounding pressure outside the vein, the vessel collapses to a
dumbbell shape.

column1:
The great saphenous vein is attached to the _______ aspect of the common femoral vein.

A: posterior
B: medial
C: anterior
D: lateral
column2:
Answer: B. medial

Feedback:
The great saphenous vein is attached to the medial aspect of the common femoral vein.

column1:
The transducer is placed in the longitudinal position slightly anterior to midline of the medial upper left calf. The beam is angled posterior until the image displayed appears. What vessel is indicated by letter B?

A: peroneal vein
B: gastrocnemius vein
C: anterior tibial vein
D: posterior tibial vein
column2:
Answer: A. peroneal vein

Feedback:
The image displays the confluence of the posterior tibial and peroneal veins. The peroneal veins are deeper on the image.

column1:
The image is a transverse view of the right antecubital fossa. What vessel is indicated by letter B?

A: basilic vein
B: cephalic vein
C: median cubital vein
D: brachial vein
column2:
Answer: A. basilic vein

Feedback:
If the image is of the right arm, the left side of the image corresponds with the lateral right arm. The veins of the arm listed from
lateral to medial: cephalic, brachial, basilic

column1:
The small saphenous vein:

A: originates posterior to the medial malleolus and ascends along the midline aspect of posterior calf
B: originates immediately posterior to the Achilles' tendon attachment at the heel and ascends along the midline aspect of posterior calf
C: originates anterior to the lateral malleolus and ascends along the midline aspect of posterior calf
D: originates posterior to the lateral malleolus and ascends along the midline aspect of posterior calf
column2:
Answer: D. originates posterior to the lateral malleolus and ascends along the midline aspect of posterior calf

Feedback:
The small saphenous vein originates posterior to the lateral malleolus and ascends along the midline aspect of posterior calf.

column1:
Which of the following veins originates at the medial wrist and courses up the medial arm to join the axillary vein?

A: Basilic vein
B: Radial vein
C: Cephalic vein
D: Ulnar vein
column2:
Answer: A. Basilic vein

Feedback:
CONSIDER THE ARM IS IN ANATOMIC POSITION FOR ALL ANATOMY QUESTIONS. The radial veins join the ulnar veins at the
elbow to form the brachial vein(s). The cephalic vein originates near the base of the wrist and travels up the lateral side of the
arm to empty into the subclavian vein. The basilic vein originates at the medial wrist and courses up the medial arm to join the
axillary vein,

column1:
Which vein is best evaluated for compressibility by asking the patient to perform a quick, deep sniff?

A: axillary vein
B: subclavian vein
C: distal IVC
D: great saphenous vein
column2:
Answer: B. subclavian vein

Feedback:
Manual compression of the subclavian vein is limited due to its position beneath the clavicle. If the patient performs a quick,
deep sniff, the normal subclavian vein should demonstrate compression.

column1:
While performing a lower extremity venous exam, you identify a small pair of veins with an accompanying artery deep in the calf muscle, that connect to the popliteal vein. What veins are these?

A: anterior tibial veins
B: sural veins
C: posterior arch veins
D: veins of Giacomini
column2:
Answer: B. sural veins

Feedback:
Sural veins are also called the gastrocnemius veins. They are located deep in the calf muscle and course alongside their
accompanying artery. The sural veins empty their blood into the popliteal vein.

column1:
If the image displayed is a transverse view of the posterior right lower leg, Letter A correspond to:

A: posterior tibial vein
B: great saphenous vein
C: medial gastrocnemius vein
D: small saphenous vein
column2:
Answer: D. small saphenous vein

Feedback:
When imaging the posterior leg, the notch on the transducer should be placed toward the right side of the patient, just like
imaging the anterior leg. The right side of the image is lateral and the left side of the image is medial in both imaging views.
A. small saphenous vein
B. lateral gastrocnemius veins
C. medial gastrocnemius veins
D. popliteal vein

column1:
Which of the following can be found immediately posterior to the inguinal ligament?

A: common femoral vein and artery
B: saphenopopliteal junction
C: profunda femoral vein
D: popliteal vein and artery
column2:
Answer: A. common femoral vein and artery

Feedback:
The external iliac artery becomes the common femoral artery at the inguinal ligament. The CFA and CFV course posterior to the
inguinal ligament. The GSV joins the CFV near the ligament. The FV and PFV join to form the CFV just distal to the ligament. The
saphenopopliteal junction is located in the upper calf or popliteal fossa in most patients

column1:
Respiration and ____________________ are responsible for venous blood flow.

A: vessel recoil
B: Valsalva maneuvers
C: the calf muscle pump
D: the tunica media layer of the wall
column2:
Answer: C. the calf muscle pump

Feedback:
Respiration and the calf muscle pump are responsible for moving venous blood through the body.

column1:
Which of the following correctly describes the venous system of the upper extremity?

A: Effort thrombosis refers to clot in the veins of the hand caused by repetitive wrist motion
B: Patients with May Thurner syndrome have an increased risk of developing upper extremity thrombosis
C: The veins of the upper extremity have nearly twice the number of valves compared to the lower extremity
D: The superficial venous system of the arm is the primary drainage system of the extremity
column2:
Answer: D. The superficial venous system of the arm is the primary drainage system of the extremity

Feedback:
The superficial venous system of the arm is the primary drainage system of the extremity. The deep system is the primary
drainage system in the legs. The veins of the upper extremity have far fewer valves than the lower extremity. They do not need
as many because the arm is closer to the heart and experiences lower hydrostatic pressures. May Thurner syndrome affects the
left common iliac vein. Effort thrombosis refers to thrombus formation at the shoulder caused by repetitive shoulder movement
that compresses the vein in the thoracic outlet.

column1:
Which of the following veins is located deep to the muscular fascia in the proximal arm and superficial to the fascia in the forearm?

A: basilic
B: brachial
C: cephalic
D: median cubital
column2:
Answer: A. basilic

Feedback:
The basilic vein originates at the wrist and courses above to the muscular fascia. It penetrates the muscular fascia just above the
elbow and courses cephalad, beneath the fascia, to join the axillary vein.

column1:
The attached image is taken from a lower extremity venous Doppler exam. This flow pattern would be abnormal for which of the following veins?

A: posterior tibial veins
B: anterior tibial veins
C: popliteal vein
D: great saphenous vein
column2:
Answer: C. popliteal vein

Feedback:
The deep calf veins and superficial veins do not normally exhibit spontaneous flow patterns. Augmentation is usually necessary
to demonstrate patency of these veins. Lack of spontaneous flow in the popliteal vein is abnormal and a sign of flow stasis.

column1:
When performing a lower extremity venous exam for insufficiency, you locate an incompetent vein that extends from the ankle to connect to the GSV near the popliteal fossa. Incompetent perforators are also identified in the mid-calf that allow flow between this vein and the GSV. Identify the incompetent vein.

A: Paratibial perforator
B: Posterior arch vein
C: Vein of Giacomini
D: Small saphenous vein
column2:
Answer: B. Posterior arch vein

Feedback:
The posterior arch vein (AKA posterior accessory saphenous vein) originates at the ankle and extends to connect to the GSV
near the popliteal fossa. It is connected to the GSV in the mid to upper calf by the posterior tibial perforators (Cockett's
perforators). This vein usually plays a major role in disease caused by venous stasis.

column1:
Venous perforators connect the deep and superficial systems of the leg at many locations including:

A: groin only
B: groin and knee only
C: groin and ankle only
D: groin, knee, ankle
column2:
Answer: D. groin, knee, ankle

Feedback:
Venous perforators connect the deep and superficial systems of the leg at many locations including the groin, thigh, knee, calf,
and ankle.

column1:
Venous valve leaflets:

A: are composed of muscle cells.
B: are extensions of the vessel's intimal layer.
C: are small flaps of connective tissue within the veins.
D: are muscular flaps that control backflow within the venous lumen
column2:
Answer: B. are extensions of the vessel's intimal layer.

Feedback:
Venous valve leaflets are extensions of the vessel's intimal layer.

column1:
_______________ reflux will be normally seen in the lower extremity superficial system.

A: No (0s)
B: Less than 0.5s
C: Less than 1.5s
D: Less than 3s
column2:
Answer: B. Less than 0.5s

Feedback:
A small of amount of reflux (Less than 0.5s) may be seen in normal vessels with flow normalization following augmentation.

column1:
Paired veins that accompany an artery of the same name are called:

A: posterior arch veins
B: Klippel Trenauney Weber syndrome
C: venae comitantes
D: duplicated veins
column2:
Answer: C. venae comitantes

Feedback:
Concomitant veins or venae comitantes refers to a vein that is usually paired, with a vein lying on either side of a single artery of
the same name. Radial, ulnar, PTV, ATV, peroneal and gastrocnemius veins are example of concomitant veins

column1:
The image demonstrates a venous perforator connected to the greater saphenous vein. Which of the following is true regarding the image and venous perforators?

A: Flow within the perforator system is normally bidirectional.
B: Perforators are normally identified in most venous exams.
C: Compression techniques are non-diagnostic for thrombus formation in the perforator system because it is not possible thrombus to form within them due to their small size.
D: The flow within the perforator is moving in the normal direction.
column2:
Answer: D. The flow within the perforator is moving in the normal direction.

Feedback:
The flow demonstrated is moving away from the probe, according to the color map. This indicates normal flow direction from
the superficial to the deep system.

column1:
There are normally _______________ perforator veins in each leg.

A: about 25-30
B: about 100
C: about 300
D: over 500
column2:
Answer: B. about 100

Feedback:
There are normally about 100 perforator veins in each leg.

column1:
The paratibial perforators connect:

A: the small saphenous vein and posterior tibial veins
B: the great saphenous vein and the anterior tibial veins
C: the great saphenous vein and posterior tibial veins
D: the small saphenous vein and popliteal vein
column2:
Answer: C. the great saphenous vein and posterior tibial veins

Feedback:
The paratibial perforators connect the GSV and the PTVs.

column1:
Which of the following occurs during calf muscle relaxation?

A: The gastrocnemius veins fill with blood from the popliteal vein
B: blood flows through the perforators from the superficial to the deep veins
C: blood flows through the perforators from the deep to the superficial veins
D: the superficial veins fill with blood
column2:
Answer: B. blood flows through the perforators from the superficial to the deep veins

Feedback:
When the muscle contracts, flow moves from the deep calf veins into the popliteal and femoral vein. As the muscle relaxes,
blood is essentially sucked through the perforators from the superficial system into the deep system to play a part in refilling
the deep vessels.

column1:
_________________________ carry(s) the blood from the GSV in the distal thigh into the distal femoral vein.

A: Paratibial perforators
B: Lateral perforators
C: Thigh perforators
D: Posterior venous arch
column2:
Answer: C. Thigh perforators

Feedback:
The distal thigh perforators, formerly known as Dodd's perforators, drain blood from the GSV in the distal thigh into the distal
femoral vein.

column1:
The image is a transverse view of right shoulder. What vessel is indicated by letter A?

A: superior vena cava
B: axillary vein
C: innominate vein
D: subclavian vein
column2:
Answer: B. axillary vein

Feedback:

column1:
Which vein below is usually the longest vein in the body?

A: small saphenous vein
B: great saphenous vein
C: femoral vein
D: inferior vena cava
column2:
Answer: B. great saphenous vein

Feedback:
The great saphenous vein originates at the ankle and courses cephalad to join the common femoral vein in the groin.

column1:
Which of the following veins courses anterior to the tibial bone in the calf?

A: peroneal vein
B: great saphenous vein
C: great saphenous vein and anterior tibial vein
D: anterior tibial vein
column2:
Answer: D. anterior tibial vein

Feedback:
The anterior tibial veins course anterior to the tibial bone in the calf.

column1:
When evaluating the GSV for use as a bypass graft, what is the preferred diameter for the vein?

A: >3mm
B: >5mm
C: >7mm
D: >10mm
column2:
Answer: A. >3mm

Feedback:
When evaluating a vein for use as a bypass graft, a 3mm diameter or greater is preferred.

column1:
Which of the following statements regarding venous perforators is correct?

A: Flow in the perforators normally moves from the deep to the superficial system.
B: Perforator flow is normally unidirectional, but becomes bidirectional with exercise.
C: They each contain a single valve.
D: Thrombosis rarely occurs within the perforators and it cannot to be easily demonstrated on ultrasound evaluation.
column2:
Answer: C. They each contain a single valve.

Feedback:
Venous valves are designed to allow unidirectional flow. If the valves are damaged or the vein is dilated to prevent proper valve
movement, flow reversal may occur. Flow in the perforators normally moves from the superficial to the deep system. They each
contain a single valve. Thrombosis can occur within the perforators, which can lead to the formation of varicosities.

column1:
Flow through venous perforators is:

A: normally unidirectional from the superficial to the deep venous system
B: normally unidirectional from the deep venous to the superficial system
C: normally bidirectional to help equalize pressures in the deep and superficial systems
D: normally absent unless the deep system becomes overloaded and forces the excess blood into the superficial system
column2:
Answer: A. normally unidirectional from the superficial to the deep venous system

Feedback:
Flow through venous perforators is normally unidirectional from the superficial to the deep venous system.

column1:
Which of the following veins does not normally contain any functioning valves?

A: small saphenous vein
B: deep femoral vein
C: inferior vena cava
D: common femoral vein
column2:
Answer: C. inferior vena cava

Feedback:
Lower extremity veins that do NOT contain valves = IVC, iliac veins and soleal sinuses; Upper extremity veins that do NOT
contain valves = SVC and innominate veins. It is important to note that the Eustachian valve may be identified in the IVC at the
junction with the right atrium. It is a remnant of fetal circulation and does not function as a valve in an adult.

column1:
What venous valves are closed in the lower extremity during calf muscle relaxation?

A: All of the valves in the deep and superficial system
B: Proximal thigh veins
C: Calf veins
D: All of the valves in the deep system
column2:
Answer: B. Proximal thigh veins

Feedback:
During muscle contraction, the large blood volume residing in the deep calf veins and soleal sinuses is squeezed up and out of
the calf. The proximal valves of the deep veins are forced open. The distal valves close to prevent caudal flow. The perforator
valves close to prevent flow into the superficial venous system. During calf muscle relaxation, the proximal valves close due to
the hydrostatic pressure forcing blood back down the veins to the level of the valve. During relaxation, the distal and perforator
valves open, and blood flows into the calf deep veins.

column1:
Which of the following locations has a direct connection between the deep and superficial veins of the leg?

A: adductor canal
B: groin
C: hunter canal
D: ankle
column2:
Answer: B. groin

Feedback:
The greater saphenous vein connects directly to the common femoral vein in the groin area, adjacent to the inguinal ligament.
The superficial system connects to the deep system through perforator veins located throughout the leg.

column1:
If the image displayed is a transverse view of the posterior right lower leg, Letter C correspond to:

A: peroneal veins
B: lateral gastrocnemius veins
C: medial gastrocnemius veins
D: posterior tibial veins
column2:
Answer: C. medial gastrocnemius veins

Feedback:
When imaging the posterior leg, the notch on the transducer should be placed toward the right side of the patient, just like
imaging the anterior leg. The right side of the image is lateral and the left side of the image is medial in both imaging views.
A. small saphenous vein
B. lateral gastrocnemius veins
C. medial gastrocnemius veins
D. popliteal vein

column1:
Which of the following statements regarding a Duplex exam of the upper extremity veins is correct?

A: The cephalic vein is easily compressed with light probe pressure, which can be an exam limitation.
B: The subclavian and innominate veins normally demonstrate continuous flow.
C: Inspiration will cause a decrease in flow within the larger upper extremity veins.
D: The internal jugular vein cannot be evaluated by ultrasound due to its course through bony structures of the neck.
column2:
Answer: A. The cephalic vein is easily compressed with light probe pressure, which can be an exam limitation.

Feedback:
The cephalic vein is easily compressed with light probe pressure, which can be an exam limitation. The basilic vein is also easily
compressible due to its location.
Proper compression of the subclavian vein is normally inhibited by the clavicle. The subclavian and innominate veins usually
demonstrate pulsatile, phasic flow due to their proximity to the heart/lungs. The IJV should be evaluated on all upper extremity
venous duplex exams. It courses through the soft tissue/muscle of the neck, adjacent to the carotid artery. Inspiration will cause
a decrease in flow within the larger upper extremity veins.

column1:
When a patient performs the Valsalva strain, the intra-abdominal pressure ___________________ and the intrathoracic pressure _____________.

A: increases, increases
B: decreases. increases
C: increases, decreases
D: does not change, increases
column2:
Answer: A. increases, increases

Feedback:
When a patient performs the Valsalva strain, the intra-abdominal pressure increases and the intrathoracic pressure increases.

column1:
Increased pressure in the venous system results in:

A: an increased risk of stroke
B: decreased arterial pressure
C: Baker cyst formation
D: edema
column2:
Answer: D. edema

Feedback:
Increased pressure in the venous system results in edema as fluid is forced outward into surrounding tissues.

column1:
Where are the venous sinusoids in the lower extremity?

A: within the quadriceps muscle
B: popliteal fossa
C: within the adductor canal
D: between the soleal and gastrocnemius muscle
column2:
Answer: D. between the soleal and gastrocnemius muscle

Feedback:
The venous sinusoids serve as blood reservoirs in the calf and are an important part of the calf pump mechanism. They are
located between the soleal and gastrocnemius muscle.

column1:
_________________________ receives the blood from the posterior tibial perforators and plays a major role in venous stasis of the lower extremity.

A: The lateral perforator
B: The vein of Giacomini
C: The anterior accessory saphenous vein
D: The posterior venous arch
column2:
Answer: D. The posterior venous arch

Feedback:
Posterior arch vein (AKA posterior accessory saphenous vein) extends superior from the ankle to the GSV in the mid-calf; plays a
major role in venous stasis; connected to 3 ankle perforators called posterior tibial perforators (formerly Cockett's Perforators.)

column1:
What vein contains blood with a very high oxygen content?

A: IVC
B: hepatic veins
C: pulmonary veins
D: portal veins
column2:
Answer: C. pulmonary veins

Feedback:
The pulmonary veins are the only veins in the body that carry highly oxygenated blood. These veins drain the blood from the
lungs into the left atrium for distribution to the body through the left ventricle/aorta. All other veins carry blood that have
depleted levels of oxygen because the organs that they are draining require the oxygen to function.

column1:
The transducer is placed over the mid femoral vein while calf compression is performed. The Doppler waveform demonstrates spontaneous flow with an increase in forward flow with the calf compression. Which of the following describes the findings?

A: The popliteal and distal femoral veins have incompetent valves.
B: The popliteal vein has incompetent valves.
C: Heart disease is most likely present which causes the abnormal flow response.
D: The flow response is normal for the femoral vein.
column2:
Answer: D. The flow response is normal for the femoral vein.

Feedback:
A normal response to distal augmentation is an increase in forward flow with the calf compression and NO flow reversal after
the increase in flow.

column1:
The femoral vein ______________________ the femoral artery.

A: is usually much longer than
B: is harder to compress than
C: courses anterior to
D: courses posterior to
column2:
Answer: D. courses posterior to

Feedback:
The femoral vein courses posterior to the femoral artery.

column1:
Which superficial vein listed forms a connection at the elbow between the two main upper extremity superficial veins?

A: cephalic vein
B: basilic vein
C: radial vein
D: median cubital vein
column2:
Answer: D. median cubital vein

Feedback:
The antecubital vein (AKA median cubital vein) forms a connection at the elbow between the basilic and cephalic veins.

column1:
Which of the following veins are part of the superficial venous system in the lower extremity?

A: small saphenous vein and peroneal veins
B: posterior tibial vein
C: great saphenous vein and femoral vein
D: great and small saphenous veins
column2:
Answer: D. great and small saphenous veins

Feedback:
The great and small saphenous veins make up the superficial venous drainage system of the lower extremity.

column1:
The Achilles tendon is a landmark for which deep calf vein?

A: peroneal vein
B: posterior tibial vein
C: anterior tibial vein
D: small saphenous vein
column2:
Answer: B. posterior tibial vein

Feedback:
The posterior tibial veins are identified at the area of the medial malleolus. The posterior tibial veins drain blood from the
plantar arches and then course cephalad laying anterior to the tendon and posterior to the medial malleolus/tibial bone. The
SSV is located posterior to the tendon, but it is a superficial vein.

column1:
The gastrocnemius plexus is usually connected to the:

A: great saphenous vein
B: profunda vein
C: superficial femoral vein
D: popliteal vein
column2:
Answer: D. popliteal vein

Feedback:
The gastrocnemius plexus is located in the calf muscle and drains into the popliteal veins. It acts as a reservoir for blood when
sitting and standing etc.

column1:
Assuming proper transducer orientation, which of the following normal vessels could be displayed on the image?

A: left common femoral vein and greater saphenous vein
B: right axillary vein and cephalic vein
C: left subclavian vein and cephalic vein
D: superior vena cava and right innominate vein
column2:
Answer: B. right axillary vein and cephalic vein

Feedback:
The flow in the blue vessel is moving into the red vessel. The flow in the red vessel is moving toward the right side of the image.
This is a transverse image of the right shoulder. The left side of the image is the lateral arm. The right side of the image is the
medial arm. The vessels would be reversed on the image of this was a left shoulder image. If this were a longitudinal view of the
groin it would demonstrate the greater saphenous vein extending upward on the left side of the image.

column1:
If the image displayed is a transverse view of the posterior right lower leg, Letter D correspond to:

A: lateral gastrocnemius veins
B: posterior tibial veins
C: medial gastrocnemius veins
D: popliteal vein
column2:
Answer: D. popliteal vein

Feedback:
When imaging the posterior leg, the notch on the transducer should be placed toward the right side of the patient, just like
imaging the anterior leg. The right side of the image is lateral and the left side of the image is medial in both imaging views.
A. small saphenous vein
B. lateral gastrocnemius veins
C. medial gastrocnemius veins
D. popliteal vein

column1: Use your mouse to move the cursor over the great saphenous vein and click to mark the vessel.
column2:
Answer: Unavailable

Feedback:
The saphenous veins course within the saphenous compartment that is formed by the two layers of fascia. The fascia appears
as a linear echogenic reflection separating the layers of tissue. Tributary veins are found superficial to the superficial fascia.

column1:
Which of the following is unaffected by vasoconstriction and vasodilation in the arterioles?

A: peripheral resistance
B: the number of phases of flow in the arterial waveform
C: hydrostatic pressure
D: the amount of diastolic flow in an artery
column2:
Answer: C. hydrostatic pressure

Feedback:
Hydrostatic pressure is the weight of the column of blood in the vessels when standing. Vasoconstriction will increase
peripheral resistance and increase resistance in the feeding arteries. Higher resistance flow will have less antegrade flow in
diastole. Vasodilation will decrease peripheral resistance and decrease resistance in the feeding arteries. Lower resistance flow
will have more antegrade flow in diastole. A biphasic waveform can become monophasic.

column1:
Which of the following is true regarding the superficial veins of the lower extremity?

A: They are located 1-2cm below skin surface
B: They are more difficult to compress than the deep system
C: They do not contain valves
D: The superficial veins course cephalad to meet the profunda vein to form the common femoral vein
column2:
Answer: A. They are located 1-2cm below skin surface

Feedback:
The superficial system of the legs contains numerous valves and the veins are very easy to compress because they are located
1-2cm below the skin surface.

column1:
If letter A is the basilic vein, what extremity is being evaluated?

A: right forearm
B: left forearm
C: left distal upper arm
D: right distal upper arm
column2:
Answer: C. left distal upper arm

Feedback:
If the image is of the left arm, the left side of the image corresponds with the medial arm. The basilic vein is the medial vein in
the left arm. There is only one set of deep veins and an artery located centrally on the image. This would indicate the brachial
vessels. The image is of the upper arm.

column1:
2D transverse views of the lower extremity veins are preferred for:

A: evaluating compressibility
B: demonstrating the extent of thrombus
C: PW Doppler evaluation
D: color Doppler evaluation
column2:
Answer: A. evaluating compressibility

Feedback:
Sagittal views are preferred for evaluating the extent of thrombus formation, demonstrating color and PW Doppler exams.
Transverse views are used to obtain 2D images or clips of vein compression.

column1:
Which vein(s) originates at the ankle and courses along the medial border of the fibula to join the posterior tibial veins just distal to the popliteal fossa?

A: deep femoral veins
B: great saphenous vein
C: peroneal veins
D: small saphenous vein
column2:
Answer: C. peroneal veins

Feedback:
The peroneal vein originates at the ankle and courses along the medial border of the fibula to join the PTV just distal to the
popliteal fossa.

column1:
Increased transducer pressure during a venous evaluation can have what negative effect on the exam?

A: demonstrate compression of a vein
B: non-visualization of normal veins
C: increased beam intensity
D: increase beam penetration
column2:
Answer: B. non-visualization of normal veins

Feedback:
If transducer pressure is too great, normal superficial veins could be compressed causing non-visualization. Beam intensity does
not change with transducer pressure. Transducer compression should be performed to assess vein compression (not a negative
effect).

column1:
The image attached demonstrates which of the following?

A: Venous dissection
B: Chronic DVT
C: Venous valve
D: Venous sclerosis
column2:
Answer: C. Venous valve

Feedback:

column1:
The innominate vein is formed by the junction of:

A: subclavian vein and internal jugular vein
B: superior vena cava and inferior vena cava
C: internal jugular vein and superior vena cava
D: subclavian vein and cephalic vein
column2:
Answer: A. subclavian vein and internal jugular vein

Feedback:
The innominate vein is formed by the junction of subclavian vein and internal jugular vein.

column1:
During a color evaluation of the common femoral vein, the patient is asked to perform the Valsalva maneuver. The cephalic flow indicated by the blue color in the CFV immediately disappears until the patient releases their muscles. The blue color returns and completely fills the vessel. What do these color Doppler findings indicate?

A: The response described is a normal result for the CFV.
B: Superficial venous insufficiency is present.
C: There is most likely an occlusive DVT in the superficial femoral vein causing the venous turbulence proximally.
D: A significant level of insufficiency is present and should be further evaluated with PW or CW Doppler techniques.
column2:
Answer: A. The response described is a normal result for the CFV.

Feedback:
When the Valsalva maneuver is applied, flow within the lower extremities should decrease significantly and/or stop completely.
The loss of color flow during the Valsalva maneuver is a normal response of cessation of flow.

column1:
What patient position should be used when mapping the great saphenous vein for harvest?

A: left lateral decubitus
B: reverse Trendelenburg
C: supine
D: Trendelenburg
column2:
Answer: B. reverse Trendelenburg

Feedback:
Place the extremity in a dependent position.
Leg: reverse Trendelenburg for the GSV and decubitus for the SSV
Arms: supine with arms extended at side at a level lower than the heart or seated position with hand on lap; tourniquets can be
applied in cases where the veins are very small

column1:
What is the arrow pointing to on the image of the right groin?

A: common femoral vein
B: superficial femoral artery
C: great saphenous vein
D: femoral vein
column2:
Answer: C. great saphenous vein

Feedback:
Note the thrombus in the most anterior vessel. This vessel courses anterior to the deep fascia. Note the linear echogenicity that
courses posterior to the thrombosed vein. The image demonstrates the femoral vein and the profunda femoral vein merging to
form the common femoral vein. The great saphenous vein joins the common femoral vein at the groin. The GSV demonstrates
thrombosis.

column1: The image displayed is from the right groin in a patient with suspected venous insufficiency. Use your mouse to position the cursor over the superficial epigastric vein and click to select it.
column2:
Answer: Unavailable

Feedback:
The superficial epigastric vein is a tributary of the great saphenous vein. It drains the skin of the lower abdomen and can cause
superficial varicosities in the lower abdomen if the valves are incompetent.

column1:
Which of the following is a limitation of the 2D evaluation of the extremity veins?

A: increased amount of fibrous tissues and ligaments in the adductor canal
B: patient cannot lay flat for exam
C: location of the brachial vein
D: inability to visualize the vessels immediately posterior to the inguinal ligament
column2:
Answer: A. increased amount of fibrous tissues and ligaments in the adductor canal

Feedback:
Limitations of venous duplex evaluations:
Non-visualization of veins due to excessive transducer pressure
Location of the subclavian vein beneath the clavicle
increased amount of fibrous tissues and ligaments in the adductor canal
Patient that cannot be semi-erect or standing for exam
The inguinal ligament is not normally visible on ultrasound evaluation and it does not cause non-visualization of adjacent
anatomy.
The adductor canal contains several ligaments and connective tissues that limit compression of the distal femoral vein.
Increased transducer pressure can cause non-visualization of normal veins.
The subclavian vein courses posterior to the clavicle, which limits manual compression.
The superficial venous systems of the upper and lower extremities can be easily compressed causing non-visualization
Patients for DVT exams should be scanned in the semierect position to dilate the veins for easier visualization. A supine patient
can cause the veins to be difficult to identify.
Insufficiency exams should be performed with the patient standing, to engorge the veins with blood and encourage the display
of reflux. A supine patient could cause false negative results for reflux.

column1:
Which of the following are typically characterized as concomitant veins?

A: posterior tibial veins
B: soleal veins
C: popliteal vein
D: subclavian vein
column2:
Answer: A. posterior tibial veins

Feedback:
Concomitant veins - refers to a pair of veins that are coursing on both sides of a single artery. Radial, ulnar, PTV, ATV, peroneal
and gastrocnemius veins are examples of concomitant veins. The soleal veins/sinuses have no accompanying artery.

column1: Use your mouse to position the cursor over the anterior accessory saphenous vein and click to set the cursor.
column2:
Answer: Unavailable

Feedback:
The anterior accessory saphenous vein travels anterior to the femoral artery to join the great saphenous vein near its junction
with the common femoral vein at the groin. When visualized on an exam in a patient with symptoms of venous insufficiency, it
should be evaluated with color and PW Doppler for signs of reflux.

column1:
Which vein courses from the lateral aspect of the wrist extending up the lateral arm to the shoulder to join the axillary vein?

A: cephalic
B: brachial
C: basilic
D: axillary
column2:
Answer: A. cephalic

Feedback:
The cephalic vein courses from the lateral aspect of the wrist extending up the lateral arm to the shoulder to join the axillary
vein to form the subclavian vein.

column1:
Which of the following normal vessels will collapse when you ask the patient to sniff quickly with moderate exertion?

A: common femoral vein
B: basilic vein
C: subclavian vein
D: cephalic vein
column2:
Answer: C. subclavian vein

Feedback:
The quick change in intrathoracic pressure with a sniff should cause the normal subclavian vein to collapse quickly due to the
increased outflow into the innominate vein. The cephalic vein is a superficial vein that courses outside the musculature of the
arm to enter the subclavian vein at the shoulder. A quick sniff would not affect the flow in a superficial vein. The common
femoral vein is outside the chest/abdomen cavity. The change in pressure in these two areas with a quick sniff would not affect
a vessel in the extremity.

column1:
An ineffective calf muscle pump causes:

A: the pressure in the superficial veins to drop
B: the pressure in the deep calf veins to increase
C: flow to move through the perforators into the deep system
D: the pressure in the deep calf veins to drop
column2:
Answer: B. the pressure in the deep calf veins to increase

Feedback:
Effective calf pump reduces blood stasis/pooling, decreases venous pressure. Ineffective calf pump leads to increased stasis,
venous volume and pressure; reflux is a contributing factor

column1:
Pressure in the venules does not normally exceed ________________.

A: 50mmHg
B: 20mmHg
C: 0mmHg
D: 80mmHg
column2:
Answer: B. 20mmHg

Feedback:
Pressure in the venules does not normally exceed 20mmHg.

column1:
The radial and ulnar veins merge to form the _______________________ at the ________________________ fossa.

A: axillary vein, axillary
B: brachial vein, popliteal
C: axillary vein, antecubital
D: brachial vein, antecubital
column2:
Answer: D. brachial vein, antecubital

Feedback:
The radial and ulnar veins merge to form the brachial vein at the antecubital fossa.

column1:
Which of the following is part of the superficial venous system of the lower extremity?

A: peroneal veins
B: vein of Giacomini
C: gastrocnemius veins
D: sural sinuses
column2:
Answer: B. vein of Giacomini

Feedback:
The vein of Giacomini is a superficial communicator vein connecting the GSV and SSV. The gastrocnemius veins and sural sinus
are located deep in the calf muscle. The peroneal veins are deep veins of the calf.

column1:
Which of the following is correct regarding the brachiocephalic veins?

A: Cardiac pulsatility is an abnormal Doppler finding in the brachiocephalic veins
B: Formed by the junction of the external jugular and subclavian veins
C: Right and left brachiocephalic veins course anterior and medial to the right and left brachiocephalic arteries
D: Right and left brachiocephalic veins merge to form the superior vena cava
column2:
Answer: D. Right and left brachiocephalic veins merge to form the superior vena cava

Feedback:
The brachiocephalic veins are formed by the junction of the internal jugular and subclavian veins. Right and left brachiocephalic
veins merge to form the superior vena cava. There is no brachiocephalic artery on the left side. Cardiac pulsatility is a normal
Doppler finding in the brachiocephalic veins due to their proximity to the heart.

column1: Use your mouse to position the cursor over the great saphenous vein and click to set the cursor.
column2:
Answer: Unavailable

Feedback:
The great saphenous vein travels medial to the femoral vein to join the common femoral vein near the groin. When visualized
on an exam in a patient with symptoms of venous insufficiency, it should be evaluated with color and PW Doppler for signs of
reflux.

column1:
Which of the following is part of the deep venous system of the forearm?

A: radial vein
B: median cubital vein
C: basilic vein
D: cephalic vein
column2:
Answer: A. radial vein

Feedback:
The basilic, cephalic and median cubital veins are superficial veins of the arm. The radial, ulnar, brachial and axillary veins are
deep veins of the arm.

column1:
Once the pressure in the deep venous system consistently exceeds the pressure in the superficial system,

A: perforators will constrict forcing flow reversal into the superficial system
B: perforators will dilate and allow flow to only move into the deep system
C: perforators will exhibit stasis of flow and thrombus formation
D: perforators will dilate leading to bi-directional flow
column2:
Answer: D. perforators will dilate leading to bi-directional flow

Feedback:
Once the pressure in the deep venous system consistently exceeds the pressure in the superficial system, perforators will dilate
leading to bi-directional flow.

column1:
The transducer is placed in the longitudinal position slightly anterior to midline of the medial upper left calf. The beam is angled posterior until the image displayed appears. What vessel is indicated by letter A?

A: posterior tibial vein
B: peroneal vein
C: anterior tibial vein
D: gastrocnemius vein
column2:
Answer: A. posterior tibial vein

Feedback:
The image displays the confluence of the posterior tibial and peroneal veins. The peroneal veins are deeper on the image.

column1:
Hydrostatic venous pressure ___________ when a patient goes from standing to the supine position.

A: will show little change
B: shows a quick decrease before returning to 100mmHg
C: increases
D: decreases
column2:
Answer: D. decreases

Feedback:
Hydrostatic pressure refers to the weight of the column of blood within the veins when a patient is standing. The pressure is
0mmHg at the level of the heart and increases more distally to a level of 100mmHg at the ankles. When a patient lies down, the
pressure in the "column" is dispersed.

column1:
Which of the following explains the increased incidence of DVT in the left leg when compared to the right leg?

A: The anatomic course of the left iliac vein leads to increased risk of extrinsic compression.
B: The left iliac vein is usually smaller than the right.
C: Left iliac vein is a common location for DVT formation with pregnancy
D: There are more valves present in the left iliac vein which can lead to increased venous stasis.
column2:
Answer: A. The anatomic course of the left iliac vein leads to increased risk of extrinsic compression.

Feedback:
Left iliac vein courses beneath the right iliac artery at the aortic bifurcation. Extrinsic compression has been linked to increased
incidence of left leg DVT. This condition is called May Thurner syndrome.

column1:
The image is a transverse view of the right antecubital fossa. What vessel is indicated by letter A?

A: cephalic vein
B: median cubital vein
C: brachial vein
D: basilic vein
column2:
Answer: A. cephalic vein

Feedback:
If the image is of the right arm, the left side of the image corresponds with the lateral right arm. The veins of the arm listed from
lateral to medial: cephalic, brachial, basilic

column1:
The ______________ course(s) between the tibial and fibular head to empty blood into the popliteal vein.

A: small saphenous vein
B: posterior tibial veins
C: peroneal veins
D: anterior tibial veins
column2:
Answer: D. anterior tibial veins

Feedback:
ATVs course anterolaterally to the tibial bone across the interosseous membrane of the calf and through a space between the
tibial and fibular head to empty into the popliteal vein.

column1:
The great saphenous vein originates _____________________

A: at the dorsum of the foot and courses anterior to the medial malleolus.
B: at the dorsum of the foot and courses anterior to the lateral malleolus.
C: at the great toe and courses along the ventral aspect of the foot to reach the lateral ankle and extend up the leg.
D: at the great toe and courses along the ventral aspect of the foot to reach the medial ankle and extend up the leg.
column2:
Answer: A. at the dorsum of the foot and courses anterior to the medial malleolus.

Feedback:
The great saphenous vein originates at the dorsum of the foot and courses anterior to the medial malleolus.

column1: Use your mouse to move the cursor over the saphenous tributary vein and click to mark the vessel.
column2:
Answer: Unavailable

Feedback:
The saphenous veins course within the saphenous compartment that is formed by the two layers of fascia. Tributary veins are
found superficial to the superficial fascia.

column1:
During inspiration the flow in the veins of the lower extremities _____________.

A: increases
B: decreases
C: remains unchanged
D: reverses direction
column2:
Answer: B. decreases

Feedback:
During inspiration the pressure within the chest cavity decreases allowing venous flow from the upper extremities to increase.
During expiration, the pressure within the chest cavity increases causing the venous flow from the upper extremities to
decrease. THE OPPOSITE IS TRUE WITH THE FLOW FROM THE LOWER EXTREMITIES.
During inspiration the pressure within the abdominal cavity increases causing venous flow from the lower extremities to
decrease. During expiration, the pressure within the abdominal cavity decreases allowing the venous flow from the lower
extremities to increase.

column1:
_________________________ drain(s) blood from the GSV in the proximal thigh into the proximal and mid femoral vein.

A: Posterior tibial perforators
B: Posterior venous arch
C: Thigh perforators
D: Paratibial perforators
column2:
Answer: C. Thigh perforators

Feedback:
The Hunterian perforators drain blood from the GSV in the proximal/mid-thigh into the femoral vein. They are also referred to
as proximal thigh perforators.

column1:
The superficial venous system of the lower extremities contains vessels that are located

A: on the lateral aspect of each leg
B: within the connective tissue between the muscle tissue
C: superficial to the deep muscular fascia
D: adjacent to an artery with the same name
column2:
Answer: C. superficial to the deep muscular fascia

Feedback:
The saphenous veins and their tributaries course within the subcutaneous tissue, between the fascial layers.

column1:
The image is a transverse view of the right antecubital fossa. What vessel is indicated by letter C?

A: brachial vein
B: cephalic vein
C: basilic vein
D: brachial artery
column2:
Answer: D. brachial artery

Feedback:
If the image is of the right arm, the left side of the image corresponds with the lateral right arm. The veins of the arm listed from
lateral to medial: cephalic, brachial, basilic. The red color in the vessel labeled C indicates flow in the opposite direction of the
other vessels on the screen. Letter C is the brachial artery.

column1:
The image is a transverse view of right shoulder. What vessel is indicated by letter B?

A: axillary vein
B: cephalic vein
C: basilic vein
D: subclavian vein
column2:
Answer: B. cephalic vein

Feedback:
The cephalic vein joins the axillary vein to form the subclavian vein.

column1:
Blood within the pulmonary veins:

A: contains a high oxygen content and flows into the right atrium
B: contains a low oxygen content and flows into the left atrium
C: contains high oxygen content and flows into the left atrium
D: is normally high resistance
column2:
Answer: C. contains high oxygen content and flows into the left atrium

Feedback:
Blood within the pulmonary veins contains high oxygen content and flows into the left atrium.

column1:
The transducer is placed in the longitudinal position slightly anterior to midline of the medial upper left calf. The beam is angled posterior until the image displayed appears. What vessel is indicated by letter C?

A: anterior tibial vein
B: peroneal vein
C: posterior tibial vein
D: gastrocnemius vein
column2:
Answer: B. peroneal vein

Feedback:
The image displays the confluence of the posterior tibial and peroneal veins. The peroneal veins are deeper on the image.

column1:
Which of the following veins normally do not demonstrate spontaneous flow on Doppler evaluation?

A: posterior tibial and popliteal veins
B: peroneal and femoral veins
C: posterior tibial, peroneal and great saphenous vein
D: All lower extremity veins should normally demonstrate spontaneous flow on Doppler evaluation. Loss of spontaneity indicates flow stasis.
column2:
Answer: C. posterior tibial, peroneal and great saphenous vein

Feedback:
Calf veins and superficial veins do not normally demonstrate flow without augmentation. (Lack spontaneity) Normally the flow is
very low velocity and not easily detected without augmentation.

column1:
Which of the following veins does not normally contain any functioning valves?

A: Innominate vein
B: Axillary vein
C: Peroneal veins
D: Small saphenous
column2:
Answer: A. Innominate vein

Feedback:
Lower extremity veins that do NOT contain valves = IVC, iliac veins and soleal sinuses; Upper extremity veins that do NOT
contain valves = SVC and innominate veins.

column1:
Which of the following is a limitation of a CW Doppler evaluation of the lower extremity veins?

A: Augmentation cannot be demonstrated on a CW Doppler tracing
B: Cannot be certain which vein you are evaluating
C: Can only be performed on patients in the supine position
D: Flow velocities in veins are too low for detection and display on CW Doppler evaluation
column2:
Answer: B. Cannot be certain which vein you are evaluating

Feedback:
It is difficult to differentiate deep veins from superficial veins and superficial veins from perforators due to lack of range
resolution with CW Doppler. The Sonographer cannot be certain which vein they are evaluating because the CW Doppler
samples everything in its path. This would make the evaluation of systems with duplicated veins even more difficult.

column1: Use your mouse to place your cursor over the normal lymph node and click to mark the structure.
column2:
Answer: Unavailable

Feedback:
A normal lymph node is oval in shape and demonstrates a thin hypoechoic rim of tissue around an echogenic hilum. Abnormal
lymph nodes demonstrate a more rounded shape, thickened cortex that can appear anechoic. and there is loss of
differentiation of the hilum.

column1:
Bank tellers, teachers, cashiers and assembly line workers have an increased risk of all of the following, except:

A: venous hypertension
B: varicose veins
C: thrombus formation in the soleal sinuses
D: jugular vein thrombosis
column2:
Answer: D. jugular vein thrombosis

Feedback:
Bank tellers, teachers, cashiers and assembly line workers are on their feet, standing still for several hours of the day. This will
cause venous blood to pool in the legs and increase pressure in the veins (venous hypertension). Stasis of venous flow can lead
to thrombus formation. Flow in the jugular veins would flow easily into the vena cava with no stasis or increased risk of
thrombus formation.

column1:
Which of the following terms would be used to describe abnormal venous flow in the deep veins of the thigh?

A: spontaneous
B: phasic
C: cardiac pulsatility
D: low velocity
column2:
Answer: C. cardiac pulsatility

Feedback:
The term pulsatility refers to repetitive flow changes demonstrated on the Doppler tracing. Veins closest to the heart will
demonstrate cardiac pulsatility due to the changes in right atrial pressure. It can be equated to a "ripple effect", like a stone
being thrown into a pool of water. The variations in flow are demonstrated in vessels further from the heart as they become
engorged with blood. If venous flow is pulsatile in the lower extremities, cardiac complications like CHF or severe tricuspid
regurgitation should be suspected.
Normal venous flow varies with respiration and has very low velocity. Spontaneous flow should also be present in most veins in
the body (without any augmentation maneuvers applied).

column1:
The soleal plexus is located:

A: In the calf
B: In the Circle of Willis of the brain
C: In the neck
D: In the forearm
column2:
Answer: A. In the calf

Feedback:
The soleal plexus is the venous drainage system/reservoir of the soleal muscle of the calf.

column1:
Where is the eustachian valve found?

A: thoracic outlet
B: near the junction of the IVC with the right atrium
C: antecubital fossa
D: popliteal fossa
column2:
Answer: B. near the junction of the IVC with the right atrium

Feedback:
A Eustachian valve is a single flap of tissue at the junction of the IVC with the right atrium that is necessary for fetal circulation.
The Eustachian valve may persist in some adults, but it has no effect on circulation. It is not a TRUE venous valve in an adult and
the IVC is described as having no valves.

column1:
How can the gastrocnemius veins and the posterior tibial veins be differentiated on an ultrasound exam?

A: Follow them distally to locate their point of origination
B: Document the direction of flow relative to the foot
C: Document the presence of a corresponding artery
D: Document the direction of flow relative to the knee
column2:
Answer: A. Follow them distally to locate their point of origination

Feedback:
Gastrocnemius veins originate deep within the calf muscle and extend to the popliteal fossa to join the popliteal vein. The
posterior tibial veins originate at the ankle and extend to join the tibioperoneal trunk near the popliteal fossa. If you are
progressing down the leg for a venous evaluation, when you reach the popliteal fossa and begin noting the calf vessels, pick a
set to follow. If they dive deep into the calf = gastrocnemius veins, if they extend to the ankle = PTVs.
Both sets of veins have a corresponding artery and flow moving toward the heart. These characteristics would not differentiate
them from each other.

column1:
Proximal augmentation during an extremity Doppler evaluation is used to assess:

A: arterial insufficiency
B: for the presence of CHF
C: venous insufficiency
D: vein compressibility
column2:
Answer: C. venous insufficiency

Feedback:
Proximal augmentation and distal augmentation techniques are used to assess for venous insufficiency. Distal augmentation is
also used in DVT evaluation.
